dailyPhases.warmUp: Display a repeating pattern graph (e.g., a wave over 3 cycles). Ask: 'Does this pattern repeat? How do you know? How long is one complete cycle?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes pp. 1–2: A periodic relationship: output values repeat over successive equal-length intervals. Period P = least positive value where f(x) = f(x + P). Key equation: if period is 5, then h(x) = h(x − 5) for all x. Vocabulary: periodic, period, cycle.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Function g periodic, graph shown on [0, 6] rising from min 2 at x = 0 to max 8 at x = 3 and back. Find least period. Example 2: Using g with period 6, find g(14), g(72), g(−17) by shifting inputs by multiples of the period.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students draw two additional periods of a periodic function given one cycle on [0, 4]. Evaluate g(18), g(−6), g(g(10)) for g with period 8 and table values g(2) = 5, g(5) = −3, g(7) = 1.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Period identification from graphs. Evaluate q(25), q(−5), q(33) for periodic function q with one cycle on [0, 10] having min 0 and max 12.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Function f is periodic with period 6 and f(2) = 5. Find f(14) and f(−4). Show the shift calculation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the period was not obvious from the graph?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Function h is periodic with period 5. Table: h(1) = −2, h(3) = 0, h(4) = 3, h(7) = 2. Find h(−2) and h(6). 'How did you use the period to reduce the input?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes pp. 3–4: Evaluating periodic functions from tables — reduce input by adding/subtracting multiples of P. Composition: find inner value first, then reduce again. General form: f(kP + x) = f(x) for any integer k.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: h periodic with period 5, find h(h(9)). First h(9) = h(4) = 3, then h(3) = 0. Example 4: Table x = −3, 0, 1, 10; f(x) = 4, 2, −1, 7. Period = 7. Find f(8), f(−6), f(f(10)), f(7k + 4).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work: w periodic with period 7, w(1) = 4, w(3) = −2, w(5) = 6, w(6) = 1. Find w(15), w(−3), w(w(8)), w(7k + 3).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A problems 9–10: Table-based evaluation with composition and general form. Problem 9: f with period 7, find f(8), f(−6), f(f(10)), f(7k + 4). Problem 10: g(x + 10) = g(x), find g(27), g(−7), g(g(10)).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Function p is periodic with period 6 and p(3) = 11. Find p(21) and p(−9). Explain your reduction steps.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when evaluating a composition of periodic functions?

dailyPhases.warmUp: FRQ 3 context preview: 'A toy car travels on a circular track. The center is 20 inches from a wall. At t = 0, the car is closest to the wall. It completes one lap every 8 seconds. What is the maximum distance?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes pp. 5–6: FRQ 3 task model. Physical contexts with periodic motion — rotating objects. Five labeled points on the graph (F, G, J, K, P). Part (A): determine coordinates. Part (B)(i): multiple choice on behavior. Part (B)(ii): describe how the rate of change is changing.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Model problem: Clock on wall, 8-inch minute hand, center 120 inches above floor, double speed (30-min rotation). Determine coordinates for F, G, J, K, P. Describe rate of change on interval (t₁, t₂) — is it increasing or decreasing? Concave up or down?
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work Passwater Worksheet B: Steve on seesaw — 24 inches at t = 0, highest 60 inches, cycle every 3 seconds. Determine 5 labeled point coordinates and describe rate of change on one interval.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheets C–G: Prize wheel (center 30 inches, 4 rotations/sec), Boat motor D (50 rot/sec, 8 inches from center, 18 below water), Merry-go-round E (3-sec rotation, 4 ft from center), Ferris wheel F (30-sec rotation, 40 ft from center), Box O' Fun G.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: In the toy car problem, if the car completes a lap every 8 seconds and the maximum distance is 40 inches, what are the coordinates of the point at t = 2 seconds? Explain your reasoning.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when translating a physical context into coordinates on a periodic graph?

dailyPhases.warmUp: A point P on a circle of radius 5 has coordinates (3, 4). 'Can you express the vertical position as a ratio involving the radius? What about the horizontal position?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes Part I: Standard position (vertex at origin, one ray on positive x-axis). Terminal ray is the rotated ray. Definitions: sin θ = y/r, cos θ = x/r, tan θ = y/x. On unit circle (r = 1): sin θ = y, cos θ = x. SOH-CAH-TOA review.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Point P(3, 4) on circle radius 5. sin θ = 4/5, cos θ = 3/5, tan θ = 4/3. Example 2: Reflections of P(x, y) — Q(−x, y) across y-axis, R(−x, −y) across origin, S(x, −y) across x-axis.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: circle radius 7, point P(−5, y) in QII. Find y using x² + y² = r², then compute sin θ and tan θ. Sketch angle 3π/4 in standard position.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Unit circle fill-in with sin, cos, tan values at standard angles. Circle radius 10, P(6, 8), find cos θ at Q (reflection across y-axis), sin θ at S, tan θ at R.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Point P is on the terminal ray of angle θ with coordinates (−5, 12). Find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when setting up the ratio definitions for the first time?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Convert to radians: 1/6 of a full rotation counterclockwise, two full rotations clockwise. 'How do you convert between fraction of rotation and radians?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes Part II: Radian measure = arc length / radius. Full rotation = 2π, half = π, quarter = π/2. Positive angles counterclockwise, negative clockwise. Standard angles: multiples of π/6, π/4, π/3, π/2. Reflection pattern determines signs in each quadrant.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 4: Convert 1/6 rotation → π/3, two rotations clockwise → −4π. Terminal ray of θ on line y = −3x in QII: choose P(−1, 3), r = √10, sin θ = 3/√10, cos θ = −1/√10, tan θ = −3.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students convert 3/4 rotation and 7/8 rotation to radians. Sketch angles 2π/3, 5π/4, 11π/6, −π/3 in standard position and identify quadrant.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A (cont.): Evaluation at standard angles. Terminal ray on y = −5x/7 in QII — find exact sin, cos, tan values. Convert rotations to radians.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: The terminal ray of θ lies on y = −(5/7)x in quadrant II. Find exact values of sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when choosing a point on the terminal ray?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Point P on unit circle at (1/2, √3/2). Find sin θ, cos θ, and tan θ. 'What angle corresponds to these coordinates?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes Part III: Tangent = sin θ / cos θ = y/x. Tangent is the slope of the terminal ray. tan(π/6) = √3/3, tan(π/4) = 1, tan(π/3) = √3. Tangent undefined when cos θ = 0 (at π/2, 3π/2).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Evaluate tan at standard angles using sin/cos division. tan(π/6) = (1/2)/(√3/2) = √3/3. tan(π/2) = 1/0 = undefined. Terminal ray on line y = mx gives tan θ = m directly.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students evaluate tan at 6 standard angles. Given tan θ = −3 and θ in QII, verify by finding sin and cos. Sketch angles and confirm tangent equals slope.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Tangent evaluation at standard and non-standard angles. Angle sketching problems. Terminal ray on various lines y = mx.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Evaluate tan(5π/4) without a calculator. Show which quadrant the angle is in and explain why tangent is positive or negative there.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when tangent was undefined?

dailyPhases.warmUp: An equilateral triangle with side length 1 is inscribed in the unit circle. 'Can you find the exact coordinates of the vertex at angle π/6? What about π/4?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes: Geometric derivation of special values. For π/6: vertex at height y = 1/2, x = √(1 − 1/4) = √3/2. For π/4: isosceles right triangle, x = y = √2/2. For π/3: symmetry gives cos(π/3) = 1/2, sin(π/3) = √3/2. These three QI pairs are the foundation.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: sin(2π/3) — QII, reference angle π/3, sine positive → √3/2. Example 2: cos(5π/4) — QIII, reference angle π/4, cosine negative → −√2/2. Example 3: sin(7π/6) = −1/2, cos(11π/6) = √3/2, sin(3π/2) = −1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students find sin(5π/6) and cos(5π/6) — QII, ref π/6. Find cos(4π/3) — QIII, ref π/3. If cos θ = 3/5 in QIV, find sin θ using Pythagorean identity.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheets A–B: Find exact values sin(5π/3), cos(3π/4), sin(π/3), cos(7π/6), tan(11π/6), cos(2π/3), sin(7π/4), cos(π/6). Circle radius 9, isosceles right triangle at π/4, find coordinates.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the exact values of sin(5π/3), cos(2π/3), and sin(7π/6). Show reference angle and quadrant sign for each.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when deriving the exact coordinates from the geometric construction?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Evaluate sin(π/6), cos(π/4), tan(π/3) from memory. 'How did you remember these? What pattern connects them?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater notes: Reference angle method for any quadrant. Reference angle = acute angle between terminal ray and x-axis. All Students Take Calculus (A-S-T-C): QI all positive, QII sin positive, QIII tan positive, QIV cos positive. Pythagorean identity: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 for all θ.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 4: Point P on circle radius 4, equilateral triangle, angle 2π/3. P = (4cos(2π/3), 4sin(2π/3)) = (4 · (−1/2), 4 · (√3/2)) = (−2, 2√3). If sin θ = −√3/2 in QIII, find cos θ: cos² = 1 − 3/4 = 1/4, cos is negative in QIII → −1/2.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students evaluate 8 trig expressions using reference angles and quadrant signs. If cos θ = 3/5 in QIV, find sin θ. Find all angles in [0, 2π) where sin θ = 1/2.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet C: Evaluating in all quadrants. sin θ = −√3/2 in QIII, find cos θ. Circle radius 9, point at angle π/3, find coordinates.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all angles in [0, 2π) where sin θ = 1/2. Explain why there are two solutions.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the reference angle was in QIII or QIV?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Plot sin θ for θ from 0 to 2π using known values: sin 0 = 0, sin(π/6) = 1/2, sin(π/2) = 1, sin(π) = 0, sin(3π/2) = −1, sin(2π) = 0. 'What shape emerges?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-4: y = sin(x): starts at origin, rises to 1 at π/2, crosses zero at π, falls to −1 at 3π/2, returns to 0 at 2π. y = cos(x): starts at 1, crosses zero at π/2, reaches −1 at π. Key vocabulary: midline = (max + min)/2, amplitude = (max − min)/2, period = horizontal length of one cycle, frequency = 1/period.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Sinusoidal function h with max 8, min −2. Midline y = 3, amplitude = 5. Max at π and next min at 3π: period = 2(3π − π) = 4π, frequency = 1/(4π).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: sinusoidal graph has max 12 at θ = 0 and min 4 at θ = π. Find midline, amplitude, period, frequency. A function g has min at (π/2, 0) and max at (π, 1). Find midline and amplitude.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater independent practice: Find period, frequency, amplitude, midline for graphs with (a) max 6, min −4, cycle [0, 2π]; (b) max 3, min 1, cycle [0, π]. k(θ) has min at (π, −10) and max at (4π, 0). Find all four parameters.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A sinusoidal function has a maximum at (2, 10) and the next minimum at (6, 2). Find the amplitude and midline.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when computing the period from max-to-min distance?

dailyPhases.warmUp: On the interval (π, 3π/2), is sin θ increasing or decreasing? Is it concave up or concave down? 'How do you tell from the shape of the wave?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-4 (continued): Concavity pattern — sinusoidal graph is concave down above the midline, concave up below the midline. cos θ = sin(θ + π/2): cosine is a horizontal shift of sine. Frequency = 1/P describes cycles per unit of input.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: f(θ) = sin θ on (0, π/2) — increasing and concave down (bending toward maximum). Example 3: f on (π/2, π) — decreasing and concave down (above midline, curving away from peak). Describe cos θ on (0, π/2): decreasing, concave down, positive.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students describe behavior of sin θ on (π, 3π/2): decreasing, concave up (below midline heading toward minimum). Describe cos θ on (π/2, π): decreasing, concave up, negative.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater independent practice: Describe behavior of f(θ) = sin θ on each interval: (a) (0, π/2), (b) (π/2, π), (c) (π, 3π/2), (d) (3π/2, 2π). Verify cos has period 2π, amplitude 1, midline y = 0.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Describe the behavior of sin θ on (3π/2, 2π). Use direction (increasing/decreasing) and concavity to give a complete description.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when combining direction and concavity into a single phrase?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Is every repeating pattern sinusoidal? Consider a square wave that alternates between 1 and −1. 'Is this sinusoidal? Why or why not?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-5: Sinusoidal function = transformation of sin θ. cos θ = sin(θ + π/2) — cosine qualifies. Four defining characteristics: midline, amplitude, period, frequency. Not all periodic functions are sinusoidal — square waves and sawtooth waves are periodic but not smooth transformations of sine. Four-phrase behavior: increasing at increasing rate (concave up + increasing), increasing at decreasing rate (concave down + increasing), decreasing at increasing rate (concave down + decreasing), decreasing at decreasing rate (concave up + decreasing).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: h has max at (π, 14) and min at (3π/2, 6). Midline = 10, amplitude = 4, period = 2(π/2) = π, frequency = 1/π. Example 3: Square wave is periodic but not sinusoidal — sharp corners, not smooth.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: g has max at (π/3, 9) and min at (2π/3, 1). Find midline and amplitude. Describe sin θ on (π/2, π): decreasing (1 to 0), concave down (above midline) → 'decreasing at a decreasing rate.' Explain why cos θ is sinusoidal but sin(θ²) is not.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater independent practice: Describe f(θ) = sin θ on (0, π/2), (π/2, π), (π, 3π/2), (3π/2, 2π). p has min at (0, 2) and max at (π, 8). Find midline, amplitude, period, frequency. 'Decreasing at a decreasing rate' — is the function above or below the midline?
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A function has max at (π, 14) and min at (3π/2, 6). Find the midline and amplitude. Is this function necessarily sinusoidal?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when distinguishing between periodic and sinusoidal?

periods.notes: Quiz on Topics 3.1–3.3: periodic phenomena, sine/cosine/tangent definitions, and sine/cosine function values

nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Local quiz aligned to CED competencies 3.1.A, 3.2.A, 3.3.A and Passwater Worksheets A–C

cedTopic.title: Cumulative Review: Periodic Phenomena through Sinusoidal Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: Quick-fire review: (1) f periodic with period 8, f(3) = 5, find f(19). (2) Find sin(5π/4). (3) A sinusoidal function has max 10 at x = 0 and min 2 at x = π. Find the midline.
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Cumulative review connecting Topics 3.1–3.5. Periodic phenomena → unit circle trig values → reference angles → sinusoidal graphs → sinusoidal functions. Each builds on the previous: periodic patterns motivate trig, trig values create graphs, graphs define sinusoidal characteristics.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Mixed example set: (1) Periodic table evaluation with composition. (2) Evaluate cos(11π/6) using reference angle. (3) Find sinusoidal parameters from max/min: max 7 at x = 1, min −3 at x = 4 → midline 2, amplitude 5, period 6, B = π/3.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work mixed problem set: periodic evaluation, trig value finding, graph behavior description, sinusoidal parameter identification. Rotate through stations or work as a class.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style problems: Periodic function w with period 7, w(2) = 4, w(5) = −1, find w(30). Evaluate sin(7π/6). Describe sin θ on (π, 3π/2). A sinusoidal function has max at (π/4, 8) and min at (3π/4, 2). Find midline, amplitude, period.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A periodic function has period 6 and f(1) = 3. Evaluate f(25) and explain how the periodic property connects to the unit circle values you used this week.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when switching between periodic evaluation and trig function evaluation?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Start with f(θ) = sin θ. Change to g(θ) = 3 sin(2θ) + 1. 'What do the numbers 3, 2, and 1 do to the graph?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-6: General sinusoidal form h(t) = a sin(bt + c) + d. Parameter table: |a| = amplitude, 2π/|b| = period, −c/b = phase shift, d = midline. To determine from graph: a = (max − min)/2, d = (max + min)/2, b = 2π/P, c from maximum location: sin(bt₀ + c) = 1 at maximum t₀.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: h has max at (π, 6) and min at (3π/2, −4). a = 5, d = 1, period = π, b = 2. Max at t = π: sin(2π + c) = 1 → c = π/2 − 2π = −3π/2. h(t) = 5 sin(2t − 3π/2) + 1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: k(x) = 5 cos(2x) + 1 — find amplitude (5), period (π), midline (y = 1). Verify max = 6, min = −4. Given min at t = 0 with value 2 and max at t = π with value 10, write equation using cosine.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Find equation from max/min points. Identify a, b, c, d from given sinusoidal graphs. San Diego daylight hours model D(t) = 2.715 cos(0.017(t − c)) + 12.250 — interpret the parameters.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Five points: F(0, 10), G(3, 5), J(6, 0), K(9, 5), P(12, 10). Find h(t) = a sin(bt + c) + d. Show a, d, b, and c.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when solving for c from the maximum location?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Five labeled points: F(2, 16), G(5, 11), J(8, 6), K(11, 11), P(14, 16). 'What is the amplitude? The midline? How do you find the period from these points?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-6 (continued): Writing equations from five labeled points (FRQ 3 skill). Strategy: extract a and d from max/min, b from period (distance between consecutive maxima or minima), c from first max/min location. Choosing sine vs cosine: use cosine if graph starts at max or min at t = 0; use sine if it starts on the midline going up.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: F(2, 16), G(5, 11), J(8, 6), K(11, 11), P(14, 16). a = 5, d = 11. F to P spans 12 units = 2/3 period → full period = 18, b = π/9. F at t = 2 is max: sin(π/9 · 2 + c) = 1 → c = 5π/18. h(t) = 5 sin(πt/9 + 5π/18) + 11.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: F(0, 10), G(3, 5), J(6, 0), K(9, 5), P(12, 10). a = 5, d = 5, period = 12, b = π/6. Starts at max — consider cosine or find c for sine. Write the equation both ways.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Two five-point sets — (a) F(0, 12), G(5, 9), J(10, 6), K(15, 9), P(20, 12); (b) F(π/2, 6), G(3π/4, 1), J(π, −4), K(5π/4, 1), P(3π/2, 6). Write h(t) = a sin(bt + c) + d for each.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A sinusoidal function has amplitude 4, period 6π, midline y = −3, and starts at a maximum at t = 0. Write the equation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when choosing between sine and cosine?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Describe all transformations from y = cos(x) to y = 0.5 cos(2x + π) − 3. 'How many transformations are there and in what order do you apply them?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-6 (continued): Step-by-step graphing of transformed sinusoidal functions. Order of transformations: horizontal shift (c), horizontal stretch/compression (b), vertical stretch (|a|), vertical shift (d). Graph y = −2 sin(3(x − π/6)) + 1 from y = sin(x): reflect, stretch by 2, compress by factor 3, shift right π/6, shift up 1.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: Which graph matches f(θ) = 3 sin(2θ) − 1? Amplitude 3, period π, midline y = −1. Oscillates between 2 and −4. Trace key points: max at θ = π/4 (value 2), min at θ = 3π/4 (value −4).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students graph two transformed sinusoidal functions using key points. Graph y = 2 cos(x/2 + π/4) step by step. Identify each transformation applied to y = cos(x).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet C: Graphing transformed sinusoidal functions. Describe transformations from parent function. Match equations to graphs. Graph y = −sin(2x − π) + 3 step by step.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Graph y = 2 sin(3x − π/2) + 1 by listing each transformation step from y = sin(x). Mark the key points (max, min, midline crossings) on one period.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the negative sign reflected the graph?

cedTopic.title: Sinusoidal Function Context and Data Modeling

dailyPhases.warmUp: A gear has a point P that is 6 inches from center, starts directly below at t = 0, center is 4 inches above surface, turns clockwise, one revolution in 2 seconds. 'What is the amplitude? The period? What kind of function — sine or cosine?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-7: Building sinusoidal models from verbal descriptions. Translate context into a, b, c, d: amplitude = radius, period = rotation time, midline = center height ± radius, phase shift determined by starting position. 'Directly below' = minimum → use −a cos(bt) + d. 'Directly above' = maximum → use a cos(bt) + d.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Gear — amplitude 6, period 2, b = π. Center 4 inches above surface → midline = 4 + 6 = 10. Starts directly below = minimum: h(t) = −6 cos(πt) + 10. Yo-yo on 30-inch string, 20 rotations in 5 seconds: amplitude 30, period 5/20 = 1/4, b = 8π.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Point on rotating wheel, radius 10 feet, 4 rotations in 8 seconds, starts at rightmost position. Period = 2, b = π, amplitude 10, use cosine (starts at max). Write h(t) = 10 cos(πt) + d. Clock minute hand: max at (0, 70), min at (30, 52). Find a = 9, d = 61.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Pencil sharpener handle — 2 inches from center, 2 rotations/sec, starts directly below, center 3 inches above surface. Write h(t). Spinner: radius 10 feet, 4 rotations in 8 seconds, center 15 feet up, starts at highest point.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A spinner has radius 10 feet and completes 4 rotations in 8 seconds. Write a sinusoidal function for the height above ground if the center is 15 feet up and t = 0 is the highest point.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when deciding between sine and cosine for a physical context?

cedTopic.title: Sinusoidal Function Context and Data Modeling

dailyPhases.warmUp: Average monthly temperatures: Month 1→38°F, 3→48°F, 5→65°F, 7→78°F, 9→72°F, 11→52°F. 'Estimate the amplitude, period, and midline from this data.'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-7 (continued): Building models from data and interpreting existing equations. From data: sinusoidal regression (calculator, 16 iterations). From equation: T(m) = 25.7 sin(π(m − 4)/6) + 61.2 — max = 61.2 + 25.7 = 86.9°F in month 7 (July), min = 61.2 − 25.7 = 35.5°F. Contextual parameters: amplitude = variation size, period = cycle time, midline = average, phase shift = where cycle begins.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: T(m) = 25.7 sin(π(m − 4)/6) + 61.2. Maximum when argument = π/2: π(m − 4)/6 = π/2 → m − 4 = 3 → m = 7 (July). Example 3: Nighttime hours table, sinusoidal regression gives F(t) ≈ 3.2 sin(0.52(t − 4)) + 8.3. Values 3.2 = amplitude, 0.52 ≈ 2π/12 (yearly cycle), 8.3 = average hours.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: In T(m) = 25.7 sin(π(m − 4)/6) + 61.2, find minimum temperature and the month it occurs. Minimum when argument = −π/2: m − 4 = −3 → m = 1 (January). Min = 35.5°F.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: San Diego daylight D(t) = 2.715 cos(0.017(t − 172)) + 12.250 — find max and min daylight hours and approximate days. Data table with F(1) = 11.4, F(3) = 9.7, F(6) = 5.2, F(8) = 6.2, F(11) = 10.5 — regression gives F(t) ≈ 3.2 sin(0.52(t − 4)) + 8.3. Interpret the parameters.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Given T(t) = 15 sin(π/6(t − 4)) + 50, find the temperature in month 10. Show your work.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when interpreting parameters in a real-world context?

periods.passwaterSource: curriculum/source/passwater/unit-3.md Worksheets D2, D3, E, F

dailyPhases.warmUp: Review FRQ 3 format: Part (A) determine 5 labeled point coordinates, Part (B)(i) multiple choice, Part (B)(ii) describe rate of change. 'What information do you need from the context to set up the function?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: FRQ 3 practice day — no calculator. Boat motor D2: 60 rotations/sec, B is 6 inches from center, center 20 inches below water line. Boat motor D3: 80 rotations/sec, B is 9 inches from center, center 22 inches below. Students build sinusoidal models and answer FRQ-style parts.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Model one complete FRQ 3 problem: Merry-go-round E — counterclockwise, one rotation every 3 seconds, B on edge 4 feet from center, center 10 feet from fence. Amplitude = 4, period = 3, b = 2π/3. Set up h(t), determine 5 labeled points, describe rate of change on one interval.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work in pairs on Boat motor D2 and D3. Each pair builds the sinusoidal model, determines 5 labeled points, and describes rate of change on a specified interval. Teacher circulates to check setup.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheets E and F independently: Merry-go-round (E) and Ferris wheel (F). Complete Part (A) coordinates, Part (B)(i) multiple choice, Part (B)(ii) rate of change description.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: For the Ferris wheel (clockwise, 30-second rotation, B is 40 feet from center, center 50 feet above ground), state the amplitude and period. Write the midline equation.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the context had an unfamiliar setup?

dailyPhases.warmUp: A context says: 'At t = 0, the object is at its lowest point. One full cycle takes 4 seconds.' 'Should you use sine or cosine? Why?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Continued FRQ 3 practice. Prize wheel C: center 30 inches from point A, 4 rotations per second, at t = 0 point A at lowest. Box O' Fun G: table 36 inches above ground, crank center 9 inches above table, at t = 0 highest point is 50 inches from ground, one rotation every 4 seconds. Connect sinusoidal equation writing to FRQ 3 Part (A).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Model Box O' Fun G: Amplitude = ? Highest = 50 inches from ground. Center of crank = 36 + 9 = 45 inches from ground. Amplitude = 50 − 45 = 5. Period = 4, b = π/2. Starts at highest = maximum → use cosine: h(t) = 5 cos(πt/2) + 45.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work Prize wheel C with teacher support. Center 30 inches from point A, 4 rotations/sec, starts at lowest. Determine amplitude (radius needed), period (1/4 sec), set up equation.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Complete Box O' Fun G independently: determine 5 labeled points, Part (B)(i) multiple choice on interval behavior, Part (B)(ii) rate of change description. Then review any FRQ 3 problems from previous day that need correction.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: In the Box O' Fun problem, what is the distance from the ground when t = 1 second? Show your substitution.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the 'center height' calculation was tricky?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Given a graph of a sinusoidal function with max at (1, 8) and min at (4, 2): 'Write the equation. Then describe the behavior on the interval (1, 2.5).'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Synthesis day connecting 3.4–3.7. Multi-step problem: read parameters from graph (3.4), identify sinusoidal characteristics (3.5), write equation with transformations (3.6), interpret in context (3.7). This is the core FRQ 3 skill chain.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Synthesis problem: Temperature data for a city shows max 85°F in July (month 7) and min 35°F in January (month 1). (1) Find amplitude 25, midline 60, period 12. (2) Write T(m) = 25 sin(π(m − 4)/6) + 60. (3) Predict temperature in March (month 3). (4) Describe how the rate of change of temperature behaves from January to July.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work synthesis problems: Given a sinusoidal graph with 5 labeled points → write equation → describe behavior on sub-interval → interpret parameters if placed in a physical context (daylight hours, tide height).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: AP-style mixed problems: Graph-to-equation, equation-to-context interpretation, interval behavior description, parameter meaning in context. Focus on multi-step reasoning connecting 3.4–3.7.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: A sinusoidal function models tide height. The equation is h(t) = 6 sin(πt/6) + 10 where t is in hours. What is the amplitude? When does the first maximum occur? What is the tide height at t = 3?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when connecting graph features to contextual meaning?

periods.notes: Test 1: Topics 3.4–3.7 — sinusoidal graphs, functions, transformations, and context/data modeling

nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Local test aligned to CED competencies 3.4.A, 3.5.A, 3.6.A, 3.7.A and Passwater Worksheets A–C

dailyPhases.warmUp: Graph y = tan(x) from −π to π. 'Where are the asymptotes? What is the period? How is this different from sine and cosine?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-8: tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x). Three key differences from sin/cos: period is π (not 2π), vertical asymptotes at x = π/2 + kπ (where cos = 0), range is all reals (no amplitude). Odd function: tan(−x) = −tan(x). Between consecutive asymptotes, tan increases from −∞ to +∞.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Graph y = tan(x), label asymptotes at ±π/2. Example 2: Evaluate tan(5π/4) = sin(5π/4)/cos(5π/4) = (−√2/2)/(−√2/2) = 1. tan(4π/3) = √3, tan(7π/4) = −1. Exact values: tan(π/6) = √3/3, tan(π/4) = 1, tan(π/3) = √3.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students graph y = tan(x) on [−π, π] and evaluate at 6 standard angles. Explain why tan(3π/2) is undefined: sin(3π/2)/cos(3π/2) = (−1)/0 = undefined.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Graphing tangent, evaluating at standard and non-standard angles. Find periods: f(θ) = tan(3θ), g(θ) = 5 tan(θ/4) − 2. Find exact values: tan(17π/6), tan(3π/4), tan(2π/3), tan(5π/3), tan(11π/6), tan(π).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all vertical asymptotes of y = tan(x) on [−2π, 2π]. List them explicitly.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the tangent went to infinity?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Find the period and vertical asymptotes of f(θ) = 3 tan(2θ − π). 'How do you handle the horizontal transformation?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-8 (continued): Transformed tangent f(θ) = a tan(b(θ − c)) + d. Period = π/|b| (not 2π/|b|). Vertical asymptotes shift by c. Midline at y = d. No amplitude — range is still all reals. Find asymptotes: set b(θ − c) = π/2 + kπ and solve for θ.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: g(θ) = 2 tan(θ − 1/3). Asymptotes at θ = π/2 + kπ + 1/3. Example 4: f(θ) = 3 tan(2θ − π). Period = π/2. Asymptotes: 2θ − π = π/2 + kπ → θ = 3π/4 + kπ/2.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Find vertical asymptotes of f(θ) = tan(2θ) on [0, 2π]. Asymptotes at 2θ = π/2 + kπ → θ = π/4 + kπ/2: θ = π/4, 3π/4, 5π/4, 7π/4. Find asymptotes of f(θ) = tan(θ/2).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Finding asymptotes of transformed tangent functions. Finding periods of complex tangent functions. Evaluating tangent at non-standard angles using sin/cos division.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the period and all vertical asymptotes on [0, 2π] for f(θ) = tan(θ/2).
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when solving for the asymptote locations of a transformed tangent?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Evaluate arcsin(1/2). 'What angle has a sine of 1/2? But there are two such angles on [0, 2π) — which one does arcsin return?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-9: Inverse trig with restricted domains. arcsin: domain [−1, 1], range [−π/2, π/2]. arccos: domain [−1, 1], range [0, π]. arctan: domain all reals, range (−π/2, π/2). Critical: sin⁻¹(x) ≠ 1/sin(x). Output is always an angle. sin⁻¹(1/2) = π/6 (only one value). Common error: sin⁻¹(−√3/2) ≠ 4π/3 (outside range), correct answer is −π/3.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: cos⁻¹(−√2/2) — need angle in [0, π] with cosine = −√2/2. Unit circle gives 3π/4 and 5π/4, only 3π/4 is in [0, π]. Answer: 3π/4. Example 2: sin⁻¹(−√3/2) = −π/3 (check: −π/3 is in [−π/2, π/2]). Example 3: tan⁻¹(√3) = π/3. Composition: sin(arccos(3/5)) — draw reference triangle, adjacent = 3, hypotenuse = 5, opposite = 4, so sin = 4/5.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students evaluate 6 inverse trig expressions: cos⁻¹(1/2), sin⁻¹(−√2/2), tan⁻¹(1), cos⁻¹(−√3/2), sin⁻¹(−1/2), tan⁻¹(−1/√3). Check each answer is in the restricted range. Evaluate sin⁻¹(1/2) − cos⁻¹(−1/2).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Evaluation and composition problems. Evaluate cos(arcsin(2/3)) — reference triangle with opposite 2, hypotenuse 3, adjacent = √5, so cos = √5/3.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Evaluate cos(arcsin(2/3)). Show the reference triangle. Explain why sin⁻¹(2) is undefined.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the output was not a standard angle?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve 2 cos(x) + 1 = 0 for x in [0, 2π). 'Is this the same as cos⁻¹(−1/2)? How many solutions does the equation have vs. the inverse function?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-10: Solving trig equations — 4 steps: (1) isolate trig function, (2) find angles on unit circle, (3) consider domain restrictions, (4) write general solutions using period. Key distinction: sin⁻¹(1/2) = π/6 (one value) vs sin(x) = 1/2 has infinitely many solutions: x = π/6 + 2kπ or x = 5π/6 + 2kπ. No-solution cases: cos(x) = −4/3 has no solution since |−4/3| > 1.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: 2 cos(x) = −√2 → cos(x) = −√2/2. Unit circle: x = 3π/4, 5π/4 on [0, 2π). General: x = 3π/4 + 2kπ or x = 5π/4 + 2kπ. Does 3 cos(x) + 2 = −2 have a solution? cos(x) = −4/3 — no, |−4/3| > 1.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve sin(x) = −1/2 on [0, 2π). QIII and QIV: x = 7π/6, 11π/6. Check: Does 3cos(x) + 2 = −2 have a solution? Isolate: cos(x) = −4/3. No solution.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Solve 2sin(x) = √2, cos(x) = 1/2, tan(x) = −1 on [0, 2π). Find all solutions of sin(x) = −1/2 on [0, 2π). Write general solution for cos(x) = 0.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all solutions of 2 sin(x) = √3 on [0, 2π). How many solutions are there and why?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when checking for no-solution cases?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve cos(2x) = −1/2 for x in [0, 2π). 'How does the 2x inside the function change the number of solutions?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-10 (continued): General solutions with +2kπ (sin/cos) or +kπ (tan). Compound angle equations: when the argument is 2x or 3x, solve for the argument first, then divide. sin(2x) = √3/2 → 2x = π/3, 2π/3, 7π/3, 8π/3 → x = π/6, π/3, 7π/6, 4π/3.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: sin(θ) ≥ √2/2 on [0, 2π). sin(θ) = √2/2 at θ = π/4, 3π/4. Sine is above √2/2 between these: π/4 ≤ θ ≤ 3π/4.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students solve cos(2x) = −1/2: 2x = 2π/3, 4π/3, 8π/3, 10π/3 → x = π/3, 2π/3, 4π/3, 5π/3. Solve sin(3x) = 1/2 on [0, 2π). Write general solution for tan(x) = √3.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheets B–C: Compound angle equations on [0, 2π). sin(2x) = √3/2. cos(x/2) = −1/2. General solution writing for various trig equations.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Solve sin(2x) = √3/2 for x in [0, 2π). List all four solutions.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the argument was 2x instead of x?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve cos²θ = cos θ + 2. 'This looks like a quadratic — can you substitute u = cos θ?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-10 (continued): Quadratic-in-trig equations — let u = trig function, solve quadratic, discard out-of-range solutions. Inequalities with sign charts: (1) set f(x) = 0 and solve, (2) create sign chart with solutions as boundaries, (3) test values in each interval, (4) label positive/negative, (5) interpret. 2sin²θ + sin(θ) < 0 → sin(θ)(2sin(θ) + 1) < 0.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: 2sin²θ + sin(θ) < 0 on [0, 2π). Factor: sin(θ)(2sin(θ) + 1) < 0. Zeros: sin(θ) = 0 → θ = 0, π. sin(θ) = −1/2 → θ = 7π/6, 11π/6. Sign chart with four intervals. Product is negative where factors have opposite signs: 7π/6 < θ < π or 11π/6 < θ < 2π.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Solve cos²θ = cos θ + 2. u² − u − 2 = 0 → (u − 2)(u + 1) = 0 → u = 2 (discard, |2| > 1) or u = −1. cos(θ) = −1 → θ = π. Build sign chart for (2cos(θ) − 1)(sin(θ) + 1) < 0.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet D: (2cos θ − 1)(sin θ + 1) < 0 on [0, 2π). Find zeros of each factor, build sign chart, determine where product is negative. Additional quadratic-in-trig and inequality problems.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find all θ in [0, 2π) where (2cos θ − 1)(sin θ + 1) < 0. Show your sign chart.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when setting up the sign chart with trig zeros?

dailyPhases.warmUp: FRQ 4 preview: 'Solve sin(2x) = √3/2 on [0, 2π). Then evaluate tan⁻¹(1). Then find the vertical asymptotes of f(x) = 2 tan(3x).' 'Which topics does this connect?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: FRQ 4 practice — no calculator. Symbolic manipulations with tangent, inverse trig, and equation solving. Problems combine skills from 3.8–3.10: tangent evaluation and asymptotes, inverse trig with range checking, solving equations with compound arguments, quadratic-in-trig.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Mixed FRQ 4 style: (1) Find all solutions of 2cos(3x) − 1 = 0 on [0, 2π). (2) Evaluate sin⁻¹(−√3/2) + cos⁻¹(−1/2). (3) Find vertical asymptotes of f(x) = 3 tan(2x − π/3) on [0, 2π).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work mixed FRQ 4 problems in pairs. Each problem combines 2–3 skills from tangent, inverse trig, and equation solving. Teacher provides feedback on setup and solution completeness.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style problems: Solve tan(2x) = √3 on [0, 2π). Evaluate cos(arcsin(3/5)). Find all solutions to 4sin²x − 3 = 0 on [0, 2π). Find asymptotes of f(x) = tan(x/2 + π/4).
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Evaluate sin⁻¹(1/2) − cos⁻¹(−1/2). Show range checking for each inverse trig value.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when a problem combined multiple topics?

periods.notes: Test 2: Topics 3.8–3.10 — tangent function, inverse trig, trig equations and inequalities

nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Local test aligned to CED competencies 3.8.A, 3.9.A, 3.10.A and Passwater Worksheets A–D

cedTopic.title: The Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: cos(π/3) = 1/2. What is 1/cos(π/3)? 'Is this a value that cosine itself can produce? What does the reciprocal do to the range?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-11: Reciprocal definitions: sec(x) = 1/cos(x), csc(x) = 1/sin(x), cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x) = 1/tan(x). Vertical asymptotes where denominator = 0: sec has asymptotes at x = π/2 + kπ, csc at x = kπ, cot at x = kπ. Ranges of sec and csc: (−∞, −1] ∪ [1, ∞). Graphs have U-shaped branches between asymptotes — use sin/cos as guide curves.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(x) = 3 sec(2x). Asymptote when cos(2x) = 0: 2x = π/2 + kπ → x = π/4 + kπ/2. Example 2: h(θ) = 3 csc(θ/2). Range: csc(θ/2) has range (−∞, −1] ∪ [1, ∞), multiplying by 3 gives (−∞, −3] ∪ [3, ∞).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Where does g(x) = cot(2x) have vertical asymptotes? cot(2x) asymptotes where sin(2x) = 0: 2x = kπ → x = kπ/2. Find range of h(x) = 5 sec(x/2). Graph y = sec(x) using y = cos(x) as guide.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Graphing sec(x) and csc(x) using sin/cos guide curves. Finding vertical asymptotes of sec(x/2) on [0, 4π). Range identification.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Evaluate sec(π/3) and state where sec(x) is undefined on [0, 2π].
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when sec(x) looked like 1/cos(x)?

cedTopic.title: The Secant, Cosecant, and Cotangent Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: Solve 4 csc(3x) + 1 = 11. 'How do you convert this into a familiar equation?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-11 (continued): Solving reciprocal trig equations. Strategy: isolate the reciprocal function, convert to sin/cos/tan, solve the resulting equation. 4 csc(3x) + 1 = 11 → csc(3x) = 5/2 → sin(3x) = 2/5. 5 − 2 csc(x) = 7 → csc(x) = −1 → sin(x) = −1. Intersection: 2 sec(x) − 5 = −1 → sec(x) = 2 → cos(x) = 1/2.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: 4 csc(3x) + 1 = 11 on [0, 2π). csc(3x) = 5/2 → sin(3x) = 2/5. 3x = arcsin(2/5) or 3x = π − arcsin(2/5) plus multiples of 2π. Solve for x and restrict to [0, 2π).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Solve 5 − 2 csc(x) = 7 on [0, 2π). csc(x) = −1 → sin(x) = −1 → x = 3π/2. Solve 2 + 3 cot(x) = 1: cot(x) = −1/3 → tan(x) = −3.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Solve 5 − 2 csc(x) = 7 on [0, 2π). Solve 2 + 3 cot(x) = 1 on [0, 2π). Find where 2 sec(x) − 5 = −1 on [0, 2π) — set sec(x) = 2, convert to cos(x) = 1/2.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Find the vertical asymptotes of f(x) = 2 csc(3x) on [0, 2π). How do you convert a csc equation into a sin equation?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when converting reciprocal equations?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Is tan²θ − sec²θ always equal to −1? Try θ = π/4: 1 − 2 = −1. Try θ = π/3: 3 − 4 = −1. 'Why does this always work?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-12: Pythagorean identities — sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (from unit circle, Pythagorean theorem). Divide by cos²θ: 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ. Divide by sin²θ: 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ. Reciprocal identities: 1/sin = csc, 1/cos = sec, 1/tan = cot. Rewriting strategy: convert to sin and cos, simplify, express in target function.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: Rewrite tan(x) · csc(x) as a fraction in cos(x): sin(x)/cos(x) · 1/sin(x) = 1/cos(x) = sec(x). Example 4: Simplify tan²θ − sec²θ. From 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, rearrange: tan²θ − sec²θ = −1. Expand (sin(x) + cos(x))²: sin² + 2sin·cos + cos² = 1 + 2sin(x)cos(x).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: If sin(θ) = 3/5, find cos²θ using sin² + cos² = 1. Rewrite (1 − cos²x)/cos²x in terms of tan(x): sin²x/cos²x = tan²x. Expand (sin θ + cos θ)² and simplify.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Simplify tan²θ − sec²θ. Simplify (sin θ − cos θ)² + (sin θ + cos θ)². Simplify tan²θ/(1 + tan²θ) = tan²θ/sec²θ = sin²θ.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Simplify tan²θ − sec²θ. Rewrite 2 sin(π/10) cos(π/10) using a double angle identity.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when multiple identities applied?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Which is equivalent to 2 sin(π/14) cos(π/14)? 'What identity connects a product of sin and cos to a single trig function?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-12 (continued): Double angle identities — sin(2θ) = 2 sin θ cos θ. cos(2θ) has three forms: cos²θ − sin²θ (both present), 1 − 2sin²θ (only sin), 2cos²θ − 1 (only cos). Sum/difference: sin(α ± β) = sin α cos β ± cos α sin β, cos(α ± β) = cos α cos β ∓ sin α sin β. Choose strategically based on the expression.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 2: 2 sin(π/14) cos(π/14) = sin(2 · π/14) = sin(π/7). Example 3: Rewrite 4 cos(2x) using double angle. Using cos² − sin²: 4(cos²x − sin²x). Using 1 − 2sin²: 4 − 8sin²x. Using 2cos² − 1: 8cos²x − 4. All equivalent.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Rewrite g(x) = tan(x) · sec(x) using sin(x): sin(x)/cos(x) · 1/cos(x) = sin(x)/cos²(x). Simplify (sec²x − 1)/sin²x = tan²x/sin²x = 1/cos²x = sec²x. cos(π/8)cos(π/16) − sin(π/8)sin(π/16) = cos(π/8 + π/16) = cos(3π/16).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Rewrite expressions using double angle. Simplify using sum/difference identities. Rewrite g(x) = tan(x) · sec(x) using powers of sin(x). Verify identities step by step.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Simplify (sin²x − cos²x)/sin(x) using identities. Show which identity you used and why.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when choosing which form of cos(2θ) to use?

cedTopic.title: FRQ 4 Practice: Identity Verification and Expression Rewriting

dailyPhases.warmUp: Verify sin²(x) + cos²(x) = 1 for x = π/4. 'Does this work for all x? How do you verify an identity vs. solve an equation?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: FRQ 4 synthesis practice — no calculator. Identity verification, expression simplification, equation solving across reciprocal functions, Pythagorean identities, double angle, and sum/difference. Connect to FRQ 4 task model: symbolic manipulations without context.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Synthesis problems: (1) Verify (sin x + cos x)² = 1 + 2 sin x cos x. Expand left: sin² + 2 sin·cos + cos² = 1 + 2 sin·cos. (2) Simplify tan(x) · csc(x) − cot(x). (3) Solve 2 sin²x − sin x − 1 = 0 on [0, 2π). Factor: (2sin x + 1)(sin x − 1) = 0.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work FRQ 4 synthesis in pairs: verify 2 identities, simplify 3 expressions, solve 2 equations. Problems mix sec/csc/cot, Pythagorean identities, and double angle.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed FRQ 4 problems: Verify (1 − cos²x)/sin²x = 1. Simplify sec²x − tan²x. Solve 2cos(2x) + 1 = 0 on [0, 2π). Rewrite 4sin(x)cos(x) using double angle.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Verify that (sin(x) + cos(x))² − 1 = 2 sin(x) cos(x). Show each algebraic step.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when an identity verification required expanding first?

periods.notes: Test 3: Topics 3.8–3.12 — tangent, inverse trig, equations, reciprocal functions, and equivalent representations

nonInstructionArtifact.evidence: Local test aligned to CED competencies 3.8.A–3.12.A and Passwater Worksheets A–D

dailyPhases.warmUp: A ship is 5 miles from a harbor at angle π/3 from due east. 'You could say the rectangular point is (2.5, 4.33), but what is the more natural description?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-13: Polar coordinates (r, θ) describe points by distance r and angle θ. Conversion formulas: polar to rectangular — x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ. Rectangular to polar — r² = x² + y², tan θ = y/x. Tip: always sketch first and check quadrant. When computing θ from tan θ = y/x, check quadrant — arctan only gives angles in (−π/2, π/2).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Convert (2, π) to rectangular: (−2, 0). Convert (7, −π/2) to rectangular: (0, −7). Convert (0, −5) to polar: r = 5, θ = 3π/2. Sketch each point.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Convert (2, π) and (7, −π/2) to rectangular. Convert (2, −2) and (−1, √3) to polar with r > 0 and 0 ≤ θ < 2π. Write complex number 3 + 2i in polar form.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Convert (5, π/3) to rectangular coordinates. Convert (−3, 3) to polar coordinates.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the rectangular point was in QII and arctan gave a negative angle?

dailyPhases.warmUp: The point (5, π/2) can also be written as (5, 5π/2) or (−5, 3π/2). 'Why does the same point have infinitely many representations?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-13 (continued): Multiple representations — (r, θ) = (r, θ + 2nπ) = (−r, θ + π). Adding 2π rotates the angle by a full turn. Negating r and adding π flips to the opposite direction. Complex number polar form: a + bi → r(cos θ + i sin θ) where r = √(a² + b²), θ from the point (a, b). Example: −2 − 2i has r = 2√2, θ = 5π/4, giving 2√2(cos(5π/4) + i sin(5π/4)).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: (−1, √3) → r = 2, tan θ = −√3/1 = −√3, point is in QII → θ = 2π/3. Polar: (2, 2π/3). Example 4: Write two representations for (5, π/2): original (5, π/2), add 2π → (5, 5π/2), negate r → (−5, 3π/2). Convert 7(cos(7π/6) + i sin(7π/6)) to rectangular: x = 7 cos(7π/6) = −7√3/2, y = 7 sin(7π/6) = −7/2, so −7√3/2 − 7i/2.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: Write 3 different polar representations for (3, π/4). Convert 3 + 2i to polar form: r = √13, θ = arctan(2/3). Convert 7(cos(7π/6) + i sin(7π/6)) to a + bi.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A (cont.): Negative r representations. Complex number conversions. Write (5, π/2) three different ways. Convert −4 + 4i to polar form.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Write three different polar representations of the point (4, π/6). Include one with r < 0.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when r was negative?

dailyPhases.warmUp: In rectangular coordinates, y = 3 sin(3x) is a wave. In polar coordinates, r = 3 sin(3θ) traces a rose curve with 3 petals. 'How does the same trig function produce a completely different shape?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-14: Polar functions map θ to r. AP exam fact: polar appears only in multiple choice — never sketch from scratch. Strategy: evaluate at key angles and match to a graph. Rose curves: r = a sin(nθ) or a cos(nθ) — n petals if n is odd, 2n petals if n is even. Negative r plots in the opposite direction (θ + π).
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: f(θ) = 3 sin(3θ) for 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π. n = 3 is odd → 3 petals. Maximum r = 3 when sin(3θ) = 1: 3θ = π/2 → θ = π/6. Example 2: Graph shows 8 petals. 2n = 8 → n = 4 (even). Equation: r = a sin(4θ) or r = a cos(4θ). Evaluate at θ = 0: a cos(0) = a (nonzero) vs a sin(0) = 0 to distinguish.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: If r = 3 cos(2θ), find r at θ = 0 (r = 3), θ = π/4 (r = 0), θ = π/2 (r = −3). How many petals does r = 6 sin(4θ) have? n = 4 is even → 8 petals.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Match equations to graph types — r = −3 sin(3θ) (rose, 3 petals), r = 1 + 2 sin(θ) (limaçon), r = 6 cos(4θ) (rose, 8 petals), r = θ (spiral). Count petals on various rose curves.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: The graph of r = a cos(3θ) for 0 ≤ θ ≤ 2π is a rose with how many petals? How does changing to a sin(3θ) affect the graph?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when evaluating at several θ values to match a graph?

dailyPhases.warmUp: A graph shows a limaçon with an inner loop. 'Which equation fits: 2 + 4 sin(θ) or 4 + 2 sin(θ)? How do you tell?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-14 (continued): Limaçons r = a + b sin(θ) or a + b cos(θ). Inner loop if |b| > |a|, dimple if |b| = |a|, convex if |b| < |a|. Circles: r = a (constant radius). Spirals: r = θ (radius grows with angle). Evaluate at θ = 0 and θ = π to distinguish sin vs cos and positive vs negative.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 3: Graph is a limaçon with inner loop — |b| > |a|. Options: 2 + 4 sin(θ) (|4| > |2|, has loop) vs 4 + 2 sin(θ) (|2| < |4|, no loop). Check θ = π/2 to determine if loop is on positive or negative axis. Cardioid (dimpled to nothing): r = 2 + 2 cos(θ) — |b| = |a|.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: A graph shows a cardioid on the positive x-axis. Which fits: 2 + 4 cos(θ) or 2 − 4 cos(θ)? Evaluate both at θ = 0 and θ = π. Identify curve type for r = 3 + 5 sin(θ) (inner loop), r = 4 + 2 cos(θ) (convex).
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A (cont.): Identify limaçon type from equation. Graph r = 1 + sin(θ) (cardioid) and label key points at θ = 0, π/2, π, 3π/2. Match r = 3 + 5 sin(θ) to its graph type.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Is r = 3 + 5 sin(θ) a limaçon with an inner loop, a dimple, or convex? Explain using |a| vs |b|.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when the limaçon classification was not obvious?

dailyPhases.warmUp: f(θ) = 6 cos(3θ) on [2π/3, π]. 'This domain restriction means only part of the 3-petal rose appears. Which portion remains?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-14 (continued): Domain restrictions on polar graphs — limiting θ to a sub-interval shows only a portion of the full curve. Matching requires evaluating at endpoints and key interior points. Symmetry: polar functions commonly display symmetry about the polar axis, the line θ = π/2, or the origin.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 4: f(θ) = 6 cos(3θ) on [2π/3, π]. Full rose: 3 petals. Evaluate at endpoints: f(2π/3) = 6 cos(2π) = 6, f(π) = 6 cos(3π) = −6. Trace the curve between these values to identify which petal portion appears. f(θ) = 6 sin(2θ) on [3π/4, π]: evaluate at 3π/4 (r = 0), 7π/8 (r ≈ 4.2), π (r = 0).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: f(θ) = 6 cos(2θ) on [0, 2π]. Domain restricted to [π/2, π]: evaluate at π/2 (r = −6 cos(π) = 6), 3π/4 (r = 0), π (r = −6). Which portion of the 4-petal rose remains? f(θ) = 6 sin(2θ) on [3π/4, π] — describe which petal portion is drawn.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet B: Restricted domain tracing on rose curves. Matching partial polar graphs to equations. Domain restriction problems with limaçons.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: For f(θ) = 4 cos(2θ), if the domain is restricted to [π/4, π/2], evaluate at both endpoints and describe what portion of the rose curve appears.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when tracing a restricted polar graph?

cedTopic.title: Rates of Change in Polar Functions

dailyPhases.warmUp: For r = 3 cos(θ), r goes from 3 (at θ = 0) to 0 (at θ = π/2). 'The radius decreased. But what if r were negative? If r = −2 increases to r = −1, does the distance from the origin increase or decrease?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Passwater lesson 3-15: Signed radius r can be positive or negative. Distance from origin is |r|. Key insight: when r is negative, increasing r (toward zero) means distance is decreasing, and decreasing r (away from zero) means distance is increasing. Distance behavior table: r positive + increasing → distance increasing; r positive + decreasing → distance decreasing; r negative + increasing → distance decreasing; r negative + decreasing → distance increasing. Average rate of change: (f(b) − f(a))/(b − a) — rate at which radius changes per radian. Use point-slope for estimation: r − r₁ = (avg rate)(θ − θ₁). AP tip: sketch r = f(θ) in rectangular coordinates (θ on horizontal, r on vertical) to visualize sign and monotonicity.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Example 1: r positive and increasing on [0, π/2] → distance increasing (point moves away from origin). Example 2: r negative and increasing on [π, 3π/2] → r becoming less negative, −r decreasing → distance decreasing (point moves closer to origin). Example 3: f(π/6) = 2, f(π/3) = 5. Average rate = (5 − 2)/(π/3 − π/6) = 3/(π/6) = 18/π. Example 4: Estimate f(π/4) using point-slope from θ₁ = π/6, r₁ = 2: r − 2 = (18/π)(π/4 − π/6) = (18/π)(π/12) = 3/2. r ≈ 2 + 1.5 = 3.5.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students: r is negative and decreasing on [π, 3π/2]. Is distance increasing or decreasing? (Decreasing r = more negative, so −r = distance is increasing.) f(0) = 3, f(π/2) = 0. Find average rate of change on [0, π/2]. On which interval is average rate of change zero: given f(0) = 2, f(π/4) = 4, f(π/2) = 4, f(3π/4) = 2 — zero on [π/4, π/2] since f = 4 at both ends.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Passwater Worksheet A: Complete distance behavior table for intervals where r is positive/negative and increasing/decreasing. Estimate f(3π/16) given f(π/8) = 4, f(π/4) = 7 using average rate of change. Compare average rate of change across three intervals: [0, π/3], [π/3, 2π/3], [2π/3, π] given f(0) = 6, f(π/3) = 3, f(2π/3) = −1, f(π) = −4.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: For a polar function, r = 2 when θ = 0 and r = 5 when θ = π/4. What is the average rate of change? Estimate r when θ = π/8.
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when the sign of r reversed the distance interpretation?

cedTopic.title: Polar Synthesis: Coordinates, Graphs, Rates of Change

dailyPhases.warmUp: Quick-fire polar review: (1) Convert (3, 2π/3) to rectangular. (2) How many petals does r = 5 cos(4θ) have? (3) If r is negative and increasing, what happens to the distance from the origin?
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Polar synthesis day connecting 3.13–3.15. Multi-step problem: convert coordinates, identify polar graph type, interpret rate of change. Connect rectangular and polar representations. Practice AP-style matching and estimation.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Synthesis: (1) Convert (−2, √3) to polar: r = √7, θ from QII. (2) Match r = 2 + 4 cos(θ) to graph type: limaçon with inner loop (|b| > |a|). (3) For r = f(θ), f(π/6) = 3 and f(π/3) = 7. Average rate of change = (7 − 3)/(π/3 − π/6) = 4/(π/6) = 24/π. Estimate f(π/4).
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work mixed polar synthesis: coordinate conversion, graph matching, rate estimation. Each problem connects 2–3 polar skills. Teacher circulates to check conversion accuracy and graph identification.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: AP-style mixed polar problems: Convert (4, 5π/6) to rectangular. How many petals does r = 3 sin(5θ) have? Is r = 1 + 3 cos(θ) a limaçon with an inner loop? Average rate of change on [0, π/2] given f(0) = 2, f(π/2) = 6.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Convert (−1, √3) to polar. Identify the curve type for r = 4 − 2 sin(θ). If f(π/4) = 3 and f(π/2) = 1, find the average rate of change on [π/4, π/2].
dailyPhases.capReflection: What adaptability move did you make when switching between coordinate systems and graph types?

dailyPhases.warmUp: Write two different polar representations of the point (4, π/6) — one with r > 0 and one with r < 0. 'How does negating r change the angle?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Cumulative polar practice — timed AP-style problem set. Coordinate conversion fluency, graph identification speed, rate of change estimation accuracy. Prepare for final unit test.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Timed review: (1) Convert 3 + 4i to polar form. (2) Match r = 5 cos(3θ) — rose, 3 petals. (3) f(π/8) = 2, f(π/4) = 5 — estimate f(3π/16). (4) Write (−3, π/2) three ways. (5) Is r = 6 sin(2θ) symmetric about the polar axis?
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students complete 10-problem AP-style polar problem set in 20 minutes. Problems cover all three polar topics. Peer-check answers.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style practice: coordinate conversions (4 problems), graph matching (3 problems), rate of change estimation (3 problems). Self-check with answer key.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: For r = 2 + 5 sin(θ), identify the curve type. If f(0) = 4 and f(π/3) = 1, find the average rate of change on [0, π/3].
dailyPhases.capReflection: What courage move did you make when the timed practice felt rushed?

cedTopic.title: Full Unit Synthesis: Periodic through Polar

dailyPhases.warmUp: Unit 3 big picture: 'What are the three major blocks of this unit? How do periodic phenomena connect to trig functions, and how do trig functions connect to polar coordinates?'
dailyPhases.topicIntroduction: Full unit synthesis. Three blocks: (1) Periodic phenomena and sinusoidal modeling (3.1–3.7) → FRQ 3. (2) Trig functions, equations, identities (3.8–3.12) → FRQ 4. (3) Polar coordinates and graphs (3.13–3.15) → multiple choice. Students work mixed problems spanning all blocks.
dailyPhases.scaffoldedExamples: Cross-block synthesis: (1) Periodic table evaluation → find trig value → write sinusoidal equation. (2) Solve trig equation → verify using identity → graph the polar form. (3) FRQ 3 context → FRQ 4 identity verification → polar graph matching.
dailyPhases.guidedPractice: Students work in groups on mixed problem sets that require skills from multiple blocks. Each group presents one synthesis problem solution to the class.
dailyPhases.independentPractice: Mixed AP-style problems spanning all 15 topics: periodic evaluation, trig values, sinusoidal equation writing, transformations, context modeling, tangent/inverse trig, equations, identities, polar conversion, polar graphs, rate of change.
dailyPhases.exitEvidence: Name one skill from each of the three major blocks of Unit 3. Which block do you feel most confident about? Which needs more review?
dailyPhases.capReflection: What persistence move did you make when connecting ideas across different blocks of the unit?
